items.title: Read the problem twice — once for *shape*, once for *details*
items.avoid: Diving into code on the first read.
items.prefer: First pass: identify the pattern. Second pass: extract bounds, edge cases, and constraints.
items.why: Spending 60 seconds on classification saves 10 minutes of misdirected coding.
items.example: Constraints (n ≤ 10⁵, n ≤ 20, n ≤ 50) often *tell* you the expected pattern: O(n²) DP / O(n log n) sort / O(2ⁿ) bitmask DP.

items.title: Use input bounds as a complexity hint
items.avoid: Picking a pattern without checking the constraints.
items.prefer: Map n to acceptable Big-O: n ≤ 20 → O(2ⁿ) brute / bitmask, n ≤ 5000 → O(n²) DP, n ≤ 10⁵ → O(n log n), n ≤ 10⁹ → O(log n) or O(1).
items.why: The constraints are a strong signal of what the interviewer / production expects.
items.example: If `n ≤ 18` the intended solution is almost certainly a bitmask DP over subsets.

items.title: Lean on stdlib data structures
items.avoid: Hand-rolling deque, heap, or sorted containers.
items.why: Less code → fewer bugs; library code is C-fast.

items.title: Write the template skeleton before specializing
items.avoid: Inlining the BFS state into a custom loop on the first attempt.
items.prefer: Drop the canonical BFS / DP / sliding-window skeleton, then fill in `neighbors`, `valid`, etc.
items.why: The pattern's correctness depends on the skeleton's invariants — preserving them is easier than re-deriving them.

items.title: Test on the smallest non-trivial input
items.prefer: Construct n=1, n=2, an empty input, and an adversarial case.
items.why: Off-by-one and base-case bugs hide in trivial inputs.
items.example: Edit distance: test `('', '')`, `('a', '')`, `('a', 'a')`, `('ab', 'a')` before the long strings.
